items.span_text: Language models have outpaced our ability to evaluate them effectively, but for their future development it is essential to study the frontier of their capabilities. We find real-world software engineering to be a rich, sustainable, and challenging testbed for evaluating the next generation of language models. To this end, we introduce SWE-bench, an evaluation framework consisting of 2,294 software engineering problems drawn from real GitHub issues and corresponding pull requests across 12 popular Python repositories. Given a codebase along with a description of an issue to be resolved, a language model is tasked with editing the codebase to address the issue. Resolving issues in SWE-bench frequently requires understanding and coordinating changes across multiple functions, classes, and even files simultaneously, calling for models to interact with execution environments, process extremely long contexts and perform complex reasoning that goes far beyond traditional code generation tasks. Our evaluations show that both state-ofthe-art proprietary models and our fine-tuned model SWE-Llama can resolve only the simplest issues. The best-performing model, Claude 2, is able to solve a mere 1.96% of the issues. Advances on SWE-bench represent steps towards LMs that are more practical, intelligent, and autonomous.
items.title: SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues?

items.span_text: Model input. A model is given an issue text description and a complete codebase. The model is then tasked to make an edit to the codebase to resolve the issue. In practice, we represent edits as patch files, which specify which lines in the codebase to modify in order to resolve the issue.
items.title: SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues?

items.span_text: Evaluation metrics. To evaluate a proposed solution, we apply the generated patch, using unix’s patch program, to the codebase and then execute the unit and system tests associated with the task instance. If the patch applies successfully and all of these tests pass we consider the proposed solution to have successfully resolved the issue. The metric for our benchmark is the percentage of task instances that are resolved. Additional technical details in Appendix A.4.
items.title: SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues?

final_answer.markdown:
Pick **SWE-bench** for evaluating coding agents. It fits your stated target — software engineering — while the other two benchmarks target different capabilities (general assistant reasoning across diverse real-world tasks, and web-environment interaction).

What SWE-bench actually tests, from the original paper:

- The dataset contains **2,294 software engineering problems drawn from real GitHub issues and corresponding pull requests across 12 popular Python repositories**. [1]
- Each task gives the model **an issue text description and a complete codebase**, and the model must produce an edit — represented as a patch file — that resolves the issue. [2]
- Evaluation is execution-based: the generated patch is applied with `patch`, and the task's unit and system tests are run; **the metric is the percentage of task instances whose patch applies and whose tests all pass**. [3]
- Resolving these issues "frequently requires understanding and coordinating changes across multiple functions, classes, and even files simultaneously, calling for models to interact with execution environments, process extremely long contexts and perform complex reasoning that goes far beyond traditional code generation tasks." [1]

If you want to go further, I can pull the data-construction and harness details from the appendix, or look up follow-on variants in the corpus.

Sources
[1] SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues?, ABSTRACT, p. 1
[2] SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues?, 2.2 TASK FORMULATION, p. 3
[3] SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues?, 2.2 TASK FORMULATION, p. 3

SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues?
tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains
ToolSandbox: A Stateful, Conversational, Interactive Evaluation Benchmark for LLM Tool Use Capabilities

arguments.markdown:
Pick **SWE-bench** for evaluating coding agents that must read a real repository, understand an issue, and produce a patch that passes the project's existing tests.

A few quick choices inside the SWE-bench family:

- **SWE-bench Verified** — 500 human-validated instances; the current default for trustworthy scoring.
- **SWE-bench Lite** — smaller subset, faster iteration during development.
- **SWE-bench Multimodal** — adds visual context (screenshots, diagrams) when the issue requires it.
- **SWE-bench Bash-Only** — restricts the agent to shell commands; useful for testing tool-use policies.

If you want a single recommendation: start with **SWE-bench Verified** as the headline number, and use **Lite** for rapid iteration while building the agent.

Want me to look up the original SWE-bench paper in the corpus for setup details (task format, Docker requirements, evaluation harness)?
